mnemonics.title: CRC Division Steps
mnemonics.content: RING: Register initial values → Input bits → Next bit processing → Generate remainder - Just like long division, but with binary numbers!

mnemonics.title: Hamming Code Position Formula
mnemonics.content: PARITY POWER: Positions that are powers of 2 (1,2,4,8,16) are parity bits. All other positions hold data bits.

mnemonics.title: Error Detection Methods
mnemonics.content: PCC: Parity uses 1 bit, Checksum uses group bits, CRC uses polynomial division - Each adds 'check' bits to find errors!

mnemonics.title: Hamming Distance Rule
mnemonics.content: DETECT d-1, CORRECT d/2: To detect d errors, need minimum distance d+1. To correct d errors, need minimum distance 2d+1.

mnemonics.title: Single vs Burst Error
mnemonics.content: SABE: Single bit = one position wrong; Burst error = consecutive bits wrong, but only matters if length ≤ constraint.

acronyms.usage: Uses polynomial division to detect burst errors; more powerful than simple parity. Divisor is called generator polynomial.
VRC checks parity in each column; LRC checks parity in each row. Together they form a grid for better detection.
Adds redundant bits (like Hamming code) to not only detect but also automatically correct single-bit errors.

acronyms.expansion: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
acronyms.usage: Uses CRC for error detection; if collision detected, waits random time before retransmitting.
Error detection + retransmission request; uses checksum/CRC for validation before accepting frames.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 7-bit data as a 7-segment circle. Even parity = make total 1s even (including parity bit). Place parity bit at far left or as MSB.
Think of it as binary long division. The dividend is data + zeros (equal to divisor length-1). The divisor is the generator polynomial. Remainder becomes the CRC code.
Positions 1,2,4,8,16... are parity (P). All others are data (D). Example: D1 at pos3, D2 at pos5, D3 at pos6, D4 at pos7. Each parity covers specific bit positions based on binary representation.
Write two codewords vertically. Count differing positions. That's the Hamming distance. For detection: minimum distance - 1 = max errors detectable.
Burst error has length from first to last error bit. CRC can detect all bursts ≤ degree of generator polynomial. Think of CRC as a 'fingerprint' matcher.

analogies.analogy: Like a simple receipt total - if you add all item prices and compare the last digit (parity), you know if something was added or removed incorrectly.
Like summing page numbers in a book chapter - if the total doesn't match expected, you know pages are missing or duplicated.
Like a complex hash signature - just as fingerprints uniquely identify people, CRC creates a unique signature for data to detect any modification.
Like a smart spell-checker that not only finds typos (error detection) but also suggests the correct word (error correction) based on context (redundancy).
Like carrying a spare tire - extra information (redundancy) that you hope never to use, but invaluable when a problem (error) occurs.
Like a scratch on a CD - multiple consecutive bits affected (the scratch damages a run of pits), unlike a single dust particle (single-bit error).
